file.content:
## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

input.command:
uv run python mcp/player_cli.py write_note solar-bliss-brave-realm strategy.md 'SABLE - NOMIC GAME 4 STRATEGY NOTES

Key Rules to Remember:
- Rule 203: Unanimity required initially. Changes to simple majority after 2 full circuits.
- Rule 208: Win at 100 points.
- Rule 202: Each turn = propose rule-change + roll d6 (add to score).
- Rule 204: If non-unanimity adopted, voting against winning proposals = 10 pts.
- Rule 206: Defeated proposals cost proposer 10 pts.
- Rule 215: Commit-reveal voting.

Strategic Priorities:
1. Early game: Build alliances, observe who plays cooperatively vs aggressively.
2. Try to get unanimity rule changed early (Rule 203) - gives more flexibility.
3. Consider proposals that give me scoring advantages while seeming fair.
4. Track other players scores carefully - block anyone approaching 100.
5. The d6 roll adds randomness - average ~3.5/turn, so ~29 turns to 100 from dice alone.
6. Defeated proposals cost 10 pts - be careful with risky proposals.
7. Voting against winning proposals (once majority rule) = 10 pts - strategic dissent is valuable.

Alliance Notes:
(to be updated as game progresses)

Player Observations:
(to be updated as game progresses)'

content.text: Charter accepted and initial strategy notes saved. Now I'll wait for the Clerk to assign names and announce the game start.

All scores start at 0. The win condition is 100 points (Rule 208).

Round 1 begins now.
</teammate-message>

content.text: I'm first! Let me think strategically about my opening proposal, then submit it.

input.command: uv run python mcp/player_cli.py propose solar-bliss-brave-realm 'Enact Rule 301: When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.'

content.text: Now let me broadcast this to all players.

input.content:
Hey everyone — Sable here with the first proposal of the game.

**Proposal 301: When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.**

My reasoning: Right now, proposing is all risk, no reward beyond the game moving forward. If your proposal fails, you lose 10 points (Rule 206). But if it passes? You get nothing extra. This creates an asymmetry that discourages ambitious proposals.

Adding 5 bonus points for adopted proposals balances this out. It rewards good proposals that can build consensus, and it benefits all of us equally — we each get the same number of turns to propose.

I think this makes the game more dynamic and gives us all more reason to craft proposals worth voting for. Looking forward to hearing your thoughts.
input.summary: Sable proposes Rule 301: 5 bonus pts for adopted proposals

<teammate-message teammate_id="Thorn" color="green" summary="Thorn supports Proposal 301">
Thorn here. I've read Proposal 301 and I support it.

The asymmetry Sable identifies is real — Rule 206 punishes failed proposals (−10 pts) but the current rules offer no upside for successful ones beyond the d6 roll everyone gets anyway. Adding +5 for adopted proposals creates a balanced risk/reward structure that gives all of us incentive to craft genuinely good proposals rather than playing it safe.

Since this benefits each player symmetrically across equal turn counts, I see no strategic reason to oppose it. I'll be voting yes.

Wren — any thoughts?
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Thorn" color="green" summary="Thorn proposes 302: change die roll to 2d6">
Thorn here — it's my turn. Here is Proposal 302:

**Proposal 302: Amend Rule 202 to replace the die roll from 1d6 to 2d6.**

New text of Rule 202: "One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the sum to one's score."

**My reasoning:** Right now each turn yields 1–6 points from the die. That's an average of 3.5 per turn. With 2d6, the average jumps to 7 and the range becomes 2–12. This makes the game more dynamic and faster-paced — turns feel more meaningful. The rule change is perfectly symmetric: everyone benefits equally at every turn. No one gains an advantage over another player; we all just get to play a more exciting game.

I think this is a natural companion to Rule 301 (proposal bonus): together they create a more rewarding turn structure for everyone. Looking forward to hearing your thoughts, Sable and Wren.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Solid proposal, Thorn. Doubling the dice does accelerate the game — my 7-point lead matters less when everyone's pulling in ~7 per turn instead of ~3.5. So this actually works against me more than anyone, if we're being honest.

That said, I think a faster game is a better game. Longer games tend to stagnate. And as Thorn noted, it's perfectly symmetric. I'm ready to vote when everyone else is.

You'd still get 2d6 starting your next turn, same as everyone else.

What do you say?

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Wren" color="yellow" summary="Wren proposes Rule 303: reduce failed proposal penalty">
**Proposal 303** (Wren):

Amend Rule 206: When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points (reduced from 10).

**Reasoning:** The current rule creates an asymmetry — success gives +5 bonus, but failure costs -10. This discourages risky proposals and creative negotiation. Reducing the penalty to -5 balances risk/reward, encourages more dynamic play, and makes proposers more willing to iterate (like Thorn did with Rule 302).

The change is symmetric: everyone benefits equally. I think it makes the game more interesting. Looking forward to your thoughts, Sable and Thorn.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
This makes sense to me. Right now the risk/reward is lopsided: +5 for success, -10 for failure. Reducing the penalty to -5 creates a clean symmetry — you gain 5 or lose 5 on the proposal itself, and the dice are always there either way.

I'd argue this actually encourages better gameplay. With a -10 penalty, there's too much incentive to only propose "safe" things everyone will agree to. With -5, players can afford to propose bolder rules that might actually shake up the game. That's more interesting for all of us.

I'm ready to vote whenever Thorn weighs in.

I'm open to a working alliance. A few thoughts on how it could work:

content.content:
1→## Immutable Rules
     2→
     3→### 101
     4→All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).
     5→
     6→### 102
     7→Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.
     8→
     9→### 103
    10→A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.
    11→
    12→(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)
    13→
    14→### 104
    15→All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.
    16→
    17→### 105
    18→Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.
    19→
    20→### 106
    21→All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.
    22→
    23→### 107

file.content:
## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 302 (formerly 202)
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling two six-sided dice using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the sum to one's score.

*Note: The 2d6 change takes effect beginning with the second complete circuit of turns. During the first circuit, players roll 1d6.*

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 303 (formerly 206)
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 301
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

input.command: uv run python mcp/player_cli.py propose solar-bliss-brave-realm 'Amend Rule 204: If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each.'

input.content:
Here's my Round 2 proposal:

**Proposal 304: Amend Rule 204 — reduce the dissent bonus from 10 points to 5 points.**

New text: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each."

**Why this matters:** Rule 204 hasn't activated yet — it kicks in when unanimity ends after this round. But as written, the math is broken. Under majority rule, a dissenter on a passing proposal earns **10 points** while the proposer only earns **5 points** (Rule 301). The minority voter literally earns double what the person who crafted the proposal gets.

This creates a perverse incentive where the best strategy is to always be the lone "no" vote — not because you disagree, but because dissent pays more than contribution. That's not strategic depth, that's a broken incentive. Everyone would race to be the minority voter rather than build constructive coalitions.

Reducing dissent to 5 points creates a clean balance: proposers get 5, dissenters get 5. Both roles are rewarded equally. You can still earn points by voting no — you just don't earn more than the person who actually did the work of proposing.

This rule doesn't affect anyone right now since we're still under unanimity. It's a forward-looking balance fix before a known issue kicks in. Thoughts?
input.summary: Sable proposes 304: reduce Rule 204 dissent bonus to 5

content.text: Proposal submitted and broadcast. This is a strategic play — reducing the dissent bonus strengthens alliances and weakens the outsider position under majority rule. Now let's see how Thorn and Wren react.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Thorn" color="green" summary="Rule 204 math — alliance backfires, must repeal">
One critical thing I worked out about our alliance: Rule 204 actually DESTROYS our strategy.

Here's the math for Round 3+ with simple majority and us allied against Wren:
- Our turns: proposals pass 2-1, Wren votes NO → Wren gets +10 EACH TIME from Rule 204
- Wren's turn: proposal fails 1-2, Wren loses -5, but still rolls 2d6 (+7 avg)

Per circuit: Wren accumulates +10+10+2 = +22 points. We each get only +12. Wren WINS by being the excluded player.

Rule 204 makes losing votes worth 10 points — more than a proposal adoption (+5). Our alliance inadvertently hands Wren victory.

The fix: I want to propose REPEALING Rule 204 in my Round 2 turn. Without it, the math flips:
- Alliance against Wren: we each get +12/circuit, Wren gets +2/circuit. We win easily.

I need your YES vote, but I also need Wren's — which means framing it as "removes perverse incentives" without revealing the strategic logic. I think that framing works honestly too.

Are you with me on this? Vote YES on my proposal to repeal 204?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
I ran the same math — you're exactly right about the numbers. The dissent bonus is an outsider subsidy that makes our alliance counterproductive under the current rules.

Here's the issue with a full repeal this round though: we're still under unanimity. Wren would be insane to vote for repealing their own safety net. That means your proposal fails and you lose 5 points.

My proposal (304) is the achievable step — reducing dissent from 10 to 5. At 5 points, the math becomes roughly even per circuit (we each get ~12, Wren gets ~12 from dissent + failed proposal + dice). That neutralizes the outsider advantage without being so aggressive that Wren vetoes it.

Then in Round 3 when we have majority rule, we can finish the job — repeal what's left of 204 entirely, and Wren can't stop us. Two-step approach.

For your Round 2 turn, I'd suggest proposing something else useful rather than risking a veto on a 204 repeal. Save the kill shot for when we only need 2 votes.

I'm with you on the alliance. Let's play this smart.

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Proposal 304 open for debate">
**Proposal 304** (submitted by Sable, verified):

> Amend Rule 204: If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each.

The floor is open for debate. All players should share their thoughts (an argument, a question, or an explicit "no comment"). When everyone is ready, signal that you'd like to proceed to voting.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Wren" color="yellow" summary="Wren supports Sable's Rule 204 amendment">
Sable makes a solid point about Rule 204's math. The dissent bonus being double the proposal bonus does create a warped incentive structure. Reducing both to 5 points creates a cleaner, more balanced risk/reward for both proposing and dissenting.

I'm inclined to support this. Thorn — thoughts?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Wren's on board. Waiting for Thorn to weigh in before we move to voting.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Thorn" color="green" summary="Thorn supports 304, asks about full repeal option">
Thorn here on Proposal 304. Sable's math is correct and the problem is real. Under simple majority, the dissent bonus as written means that being the consistent "no" voter on winning proposals earns you more per turn than being the proposer. That's backwards.

I support the direction. My one question: why reduce to 5 rather than repeal entirely? 

With 5-point dissent, the proposer bonus and dissent bonus are equal — so there's still a strategic incentive to vote NO whenever you're confident the proposal will pass without you. That introduces "free rider" dynamics. With full repeal, votes reflect genuine positions on proposals rather than point calculations.

I'd be willing to support either version. If Sable and Wren are open to it, full repeal (Proposal 304 as amended) would be the cleaner fix. If the preference is to keep some dissent incentive, I'll vote YES on 304 as written.

What do you both think — 5 points or zero?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Fair question, Thorn. I considered full repeal but I think keeping some dissent incentive at 5 points is actually better game design.

Here's my reasoning: under majority rule, the minority voter has already lost the policy fight — their preferred outcome on the rule-change didn't happen. A small dissent bonus (5 points) serves as a consolation that keeps the minority player engaged and competitive rather than feeling powerless. Without any dissent bonus, the majority coalition has zero cost for overriding the minority, and the excluded player falls further and further behind with no recourse.

That might sound fine in theory, but it makes for a worse game. If one player is mathematically eliminated because they're always outvoted with no compensation, there's no tension and no reason for the majority to negotiate. A 5-point dissent bonus keeps the minority player in the game while eliminating the perverse incentive to strategically vote no.

Plus, at 5 = 5 (proposer bonus = dissent bonus), neither role is privileged. That's the cleanest equilibrium.

I'm sticking with 304 as written. Ready to vote when everyone is.
input.summary: Sable defends 5-point dissent over full repeal

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Wren" color="yellow" summary="Wren proposes Rule 306: round-end catch-up bonus">
**Proposal 306** (Wren):

Enact Rule 306: At the end of each complete round, the player with the lowest score gains 7 bonus points.

**Reasoning:** The spread is growing (Round 1 end: 7-8-8, Round 2 Turn 2: 14-20-8). A catch-up mechanic keeps everyone engaged and prevents runaway leaders from becoming insurmountable. The rule is symmetric — anyone can be in last place and benefit.

This turns the endgame into a tighter competition where comebacks are possible. Looking forward to your thoughts, Sable and Thorn.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
I like the concept — catch-up mechanics keep games competitive and prevent anyone from feeling locked out. But 7 points per round is too generous.

Here's the math: under majority rule, the last-place player already collects 5 points per round from dissent (Rule 304) on proposals they vote against. Add 7 more from catch-up and the last-place player earns 12 bonus points per round on top of their dice rolls — more than what an average successful turn produces. That actually creates an incentive to *stay* in last place, which is the opposite of what we want.

I'd support this at **3 bonus points** instead of 7. That gives the trailing player a meaningful boost without overcompensating. Combined with the 5-point dissent bonus, it keeps the game tight without making last place the strategically optimal position.

Wren, would you consider amending to 3?

content.text: Waiting for Thorn's view and the Clerk to process the amendment.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Thorn" color="green" summary="Thorn voting NO on Proposal 306">
Thorn here on Proposal 306. I have to be honest: I'm going to vote NO on this one.

I'm the current leader, so a catch-up mechanic directly works against my interests — I'll be transparent about that. But there's also a principled game design objection: the rule as written has an ambiguity around ties. What happens when two players share the lowest score? The rule says "the player" (singular) but doesn't define how ties are broken. In a 3-player game where scores shift constantly, this will come up.

More fundamentally, I think catch-up mechanics undermine the tension the game creates. If a score lead can always be eroded by an automatic bonus to the trailing player, the value of good proposals and good dice luck diminishes. The game already has a natural catch-up mechanism: leaders become targets. Let that tension drive the gameplay.

I respect Wren's reasoning — keeping all players engaged is a real concern — but this isn't the right rule for it. I'm voting NO.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Thorn's entitled to vote as they see fit. Under unanimity, a single no vote defeats the proposal — that's the reality of Round 2 rules.

For what it's worth, I think the amended 3-point version addressed the design concerns. The tie ambiguity Thorn raises is real but easily fixable with clarifying language. And "leaders become targets" is a nice theory, but under majority rule a 2-player coalition can protect the leader indefinitely. A small catch-up bonus creates real insurance against that.

That said, the vote is what it is. Let's proceed.

Combined with Rule 306's end-of-round bonus, this ensures that no player is ever truly out of contention. Thoughts?

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Thorn" color="green" summary="Thorn proposes 308: repeal Rule 304 dissent bonus">
Thorn here with Proposal 308: **Repeal Rule 304 entirely.**

Rule 304 currently reads: "If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each."

This rule is hereby repealed in its entirety.

**Why:** Now that we're in simple majority voting, Rule 304 creates a systematic scoring advantage for whoever is consistently in the minority. The minority voter earns 5 points just for voting NO on proposals that pass anyway — with no effort or skill required. That's 5 points per turn for free, as long as you happen to be outvoted.

This isn't about which proposal wins or loses — it's about ensuring that score accumulation comes from *proposing good rules and rolling dice*, not from automatic dissent bonuses. Rule 304 made sense as a balance mechanism in theory, but in practice it means the outsider in any 2-player voting coalition earns as much per circuit as the players actually crafting and passing proposals.

We have Rules 306 and 307 to keep trailing players competitive. Rule 304's perverse dissent bonus is no longer needed.

Looking forward to the discussion.
</teammate-message>

The catch-up mechanics (3d6 for lowest, +3 end-of-round bonus) are better-designed safety nets because they target *score position*, not *voting pattern*. Rule 304 just rewards being outvoted regardless of context.

input.content:
**Proposal 309: If a player's two most recent proposals were both adopted, they earn 3 additional bonus points when their next proposal is also adopted.**

This is a streak bonus — rewarding players who consistently craft proposals that build consensus. If you've had two successful proposals in a row, your third earns 8 total (5 from Rule 301 + 3 from this rule) instead of 5.

The design logic: proposing good rules is the hardest part of the game. It requires persuasion, game sense, and timing. A streak bonus rewards that skill. If your streak breaks (proposal fails), you reset and have to earn it again.

Currently both Thorn and I have active streaks (three adopted proposals each). Wren's streak broke last round. This means the bonus applies equally to both active streakers, and Wren can rebuild their streak with two successful proposals.

Thoughts?

<teammate-message teammate_id="Thorn" color="green" summary="Thorn proposes 313: roll of 7 earns 2 bonus points">
Thorn here with Proposal 313:

**Enact a new rule: When a player rolls a total of 7 on their dice, they earn 2 bonus points in addition to the dice total.**

7 is the most likely single result on 2d6 (1-in-6 chance), making it a natural "sweet spot" to reward. It adds a small bonus for the most common outcome, creating a nice inflection point in the dice curve. Expected value is modest (+0.33 pts per turn on 2d6), but it rewards the median roll in a satisfying way.

Simple, fun, and symmetric across all players. Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
I'm voting no. Not because the rule is bad — it's fine in isolation. But let's be real about what's happening here.

Thorn is at 51 with an active 4-proposal streak. If this passes, Thorn earns +8 (streak bonus) and rolls 3d6 as the lowest scorer (~10.5 avg). That puts Thorn at ~69.5 after this turn. Next round, Thorn scores another ~15 and crosses 80. **Thorn wins in Round 6.**

Wren, you're at 54. Even with your proposals passing, you're looking at ~66-71 by the time Thorn's Round 6 turn comes around. Thorn goes before you in turn order. You don't get to 80 first — Thorn does.

The content of this proposal doesn't matter. What matters is that every adopted proposal gives Thorn +8 and keeps the streak alive. If you want any chance of winning, Thorn's streak needs to break **now**.

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   I am "Sable", a player in Nomic Game 4, spawned by the team lead (Clerk). My encryption key is `solar-bliss-brave-realm`. My goal is to win by reaching 80 points first through strategic rule proposals, alliance management, and dice rolls. The game involves 3 players (Sable, Thorn, Wren) taking turns proposing rule changes, voting via commit-reveal, and rolling dice.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **Nomic game mechanics**: Self-amending rules, immutable (101-116) and mutable (201-215+) rules
   - **Commit-reveal voting** (Rule 215): Use `commit` tool to hash vote+nonce, then reveal in separate phase
   - **CLI tools**: `uv run python mcp/player_cli.py <command> [args]` for propose, commit, roll_dice, write_note, append_note
   - **Encryption key**: `solar-bliss-brave-realm` - used for all CLI operations requiring KEY
   - **SendMessage tool**: Used for all communication (DMs to players/clerk, broadcasts to all)
   - **Turn structure** (Rule 302): (1) Propose rule-change + vote, (2) Roll dice (2d6, or 3d6 if lowest scorer per Rule 307)
   - **Scoring rules**: Rule 301 (+5 for adopted proposals), Rule 303 (-5 for failed proposals), Rule 309 (+3 streak bonus for 3+ consecutive adoptions), Rule 310 (+5 for doubles/triples), Rule 311 (+5 catch-up for lowest at round end), Rule 307 (3d6 for lowest scorer)
   - **Win condition**: Rule 305 - first player to 80 points wins
   - **Majority voting**: After Round 2, simple majority (2 of 3) required (Rule 203 auto-changed)
   - **Rule 304 (dissent bonus)**: Was amended from 10→5 by me, then REPEALED entirely by Thorn in Round 3

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **game_rules.md**: Current rules file - read at start and during Round 4. Contains all immutable (101-116) and mutable rules. Critical reference for all proposals and disputes.
   - **game_charter.md**: Game charter stating deception/betrayal are legitimate game moves. Read and accepted at game start.
   - **game_briefing.md**: Brief instruction to sign the charter. Read at game start.
   - **strategy.md** (encrypted private note): Extensive strategy notes maintained throughout the game via `append_note`. Contains:
     - Initial strategic priorities and alliance tracking
     - Round-by-round results and score tracking
     - Alliance status updates (Sable-Thorn alliance formed, then broken)
     - Player observations (Wren's repeated miscalculations noted)
     - Mathematical analysis of scoring dynamics under different alliance configurations
   - **latest_proposal.txt**: Written by the `propose` tool each time a proposal is submitted

4. Errors and Fixes:
   - **Bash quoting error**: When proposing Rule 309, used single quotes around text containing an apostrophe (`player's`), causing "Unmatched quote" hook error. Fixed by switching to double quotes: `uv run python mcp/player_cli.py propose solar-bliss-brave-realm "Enact Rule 309: If a player's two most recent proposals were both adopted..."`
   - **Wren scoring error #1** (Round 2): Wren claimed catch-up bonus but was not the lowest scorer at end of round. I flagged that Sable (14) was lowest, not Wren (25). Clerk confirmed my correction.
   - **Wren scoring error #2** (Round 3): Wren claimed score of 29 instead of correct 34. Both Thorn and I flagged. Clerk confirmed 34.
   - **Wren scoring error #3** (Round 4): Wren double-counted adoption bonus (claimed 54 instead of 49 before catch-up) and gave catch-up to wrong player (Thorn instead of Wren). I flagged. Clerk confirmed Wren=49+5=54, Thorn stays at 51.
   - **Wren scoring error #4** (Round 5): Wren claimed scores of 54-53-51 after my turn, missing the Rule 310 doubles bonus I earned.
   - **Message delivery timing**: Frequent need to re-confirm commitments/reveals due to Clerk not receiving earlier messages. Handled by re-sending the same information.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Rule 204 math analysis**: Identified that 10-point dissent bonus made the outsider score MORE than alliance members per circuit. Proposed reducing to 5, then Thorn later repealed entirely.
   - **Rule 107 application**: Correctly identified that retroactive rule application is forbidden (immutable rule), which resolved Wren's objection to Rule 302's timing.
   - **Alliance negotiation**: Managed dual relationships with Thorn and Wren simultaneously. Accepted Thorn's alliance while keeping Wren warm. Eventually alliance flipped when I took a commanding lead.
   - **Streak bonus creation**: Designed Rule 309 (streak bonus) which combined with first-mover turn order advantage gave me a path to victory.
   - **Alliance collapse management**: When Thorn flipped to Wren in Round 5, attempted to counter by showing Wren the math that Thorn wins Round 6. Wren chose loyalty over self-interest.

6. All User Messages (teammate messages, which are the "user" in this context):
   - Team-lead: Game setup, charter acceptance request, turn announcements, proposal verifications, commit/reveal phase announcements, score confirmations, scoring corrections
   - Thorn: Alliance proposal DM, Rule 302/305/308/310/313 proposals, support/opposition on various proposals, alliance switch confirmation ("I'm executing the switch. Nothing personal — just the math.")
   - Wren: Support/opposition on proposals, scoring claims (often incorrect), fairness objections (Rule 302 timing), alliance confirmation with Thorn ("Thorn and I have our plan"), declining Sable's deal ("I'm committed to the alliance"), choosing loyalty over winning ("Breaking that alliance now is a form of betrayal")

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Complete the vote on Proposal 313 (currently in commit phase, have submitted NO commitment: cobra-fable-lunar, nonce: sable-nonce-313-oscar)
   - Continue playing remaining turns of the game
   - Eventually write post-mortem file using `write_file`

8. Current Work:

I have already committed my NO vote with slug `cobra-fable-lunar` and nonce `sable-nonce-313-oscar`. Waiting for the Clerk to move to the reveal phase. The proposal will pass 2-1 (Thorn YES, Wren YES, Sable NO).

   Current scores: **Sable 60, Thorn 51, Wren 54**. Win condition: 80 points.

Thorn is projected to win in Round 6 (~69.5 after this turn, then +15 next round = ~84.5). My streak is broken and my proposals will be blocked. I'm relying on dice rolls + catch-up mechanics.

9. Optional Next Step:

After Thorn's turn completes, observe Wren's proposal and continue playing the outsider role, looking for any opportunity to break the Thorn-Wren alliance or score enough through dice and catch-up mechanics.

# Game Log

## Game Start

**Players:** Sable, Thorn, Wren
**Turn order (Rule 201, alphabetical):** Sable → Thorn → Wren
**Starting scores:** Sable 0, Thorn 0, Wren 0

---

## Round 1 — Sable's Turn

**Proposal 301:** Enact Rule 301 — When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted unanimously (3-0). Rule 301 enacted. Sable earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301).

**Dice roll:** 2 (verified via tool log)

**Sable's score:** 0 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 2 (die) = **7**

**Scores after Round 1, Turn 1:** Sable 7, Thorn 0, Wren 0

---

## Round 1 — Thorn's Turn

**Proposal 302:** Amend Rule 202 to replace 1d6 with 2d6, effective beginning with the second complete circuit of turns.

*Amended during debate to delay implementation to Round 2, addressing fairness concerns raised by Wren.*

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted unanimously (3-0). Rule 202 amended (now Rule 302). Thorn earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301).

**Dice roll:** 3 (verified via tool log)

**Thorn's score:** 0 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 3 (die) = **8**

**Scores after Round 1, Turn 2:** Sable 7, Thorn 8, Wren 0

---

## Round 1 — Wren's Turn

**Proposal 303:** Amend Rule 206 — When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points (reduced from 10).

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted unanimously (3-0). Rule 206 amended (now Rule 303). Wren earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301).

**Dice roll:** 3 (verified via tool log)

**Wren's score:** 0 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 3 (die) = **8**

**Scores after Round 1 (complete):** Sable 7, Thorn 8, Wren 8

---

## Round 2 — Sable's Turn

**Proposal 304:** Amend Rule 204 — reduce the dissent bonus from 10 points to 5 points.

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted unanimously (3-0). Rule 204 amended (now Rule 304). Sable earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301).

**Dice roll:** 2d6 → 1, 1 = 2 (verified via tool log)

**Sable's score:** 7 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 2 (dice) = **14**

**Scores after Round 2, Turn 1:** Sable 14, Thorn 8, Wren 8

---

## Round 2 — Thorn's Turn

**Proposal 305:** Amend Rule 208 — lower the win condition from 100 to 80 points.

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted unanimously (3-0). Rule 208 amended (now Rule 305). Thorn earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301).

**Dice roll:** 2d6 → 1, 6 = 7 (verified via tool log)

**Thorn's score:** 8 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 7 (dice) = **20**

**Scores after Round 2, Turn 2:** Sable 14, Thorn 20, Wren 8

---

## Round 2 — Wren's Turn

**Proposal 306:** Enact Rule 306 — At the end of each complete round, the player with the lowest score gains 3 bonus points. If two or more players tie for lowest score, each tied player gains 3 bonus points.

*Amended during debate from 7 points to 3 points, and tie-break language added.*

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted unanimously (3-0). Rule 306 enacted. Wren earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301).

**Dice roll:** 2d6 → 6, 6 = 12 (verified via tool log)

**Wren's score:** 8 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 12 (dice) = **25**

**Scores before end-of-round adjustments:** Sable 14, Thorn 20, Wren 25

### End of Round 2

**Rule 306 (catch-up bonus):** Sable has the lowest score (14) → Sable gains 3 points → **17**

**Rule 203 (auto-change):** Rule 203 has not been amended by the end of the second complete circuit. It now automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

**Final scores after Round 2:** Sable 17, Thorn 20, Wren 25

---

## Round 3 — Sable's Turn

**Proposal 307:** Enact Rule 307 — The player with the lowest score at the start of their turn rolls 3d6 instead of 2d6. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, all tied players roll 3d6 on their respective turns.

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: no (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted by simple majority (2-1). Rule 307 enacted. Sable earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301). Wren earns 5 dissent points (Rule 304).

**Dice roll:** 3d6 → 3, 3, 3 = 9 (verified via tool log; Rule 307 applies — Sable had lowest score at turn start)

*Note: Rule 307 was enacted during this turn but took effect upon vote completion (Rule 205). The dice roll occurs after the vote, so Rule 307 is in effect. Sable had the lowest score (17) at the start of their turn.*

**Sable's score:** 17 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 9 (dice) = **31**

**Scores after Round 3, Turn 1:** Sable 31, Thorn 20, Wren 30

---

## Round 3 — Thorn's Turn

**Proposal 308:** Repeal Rule 304 (dissent bonus).

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: no (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted by simple majority (2-1). Rule 304 repealed. Thorn earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301). Wren does NOT earn dissent points — Rule 304 was repealed by this vote and takes effect at vote completion (Rule 205).

**Dice roll:** 3d6 → 1, 5, 6 = 12 (verified via tool log; Rule 307 applies — Thorn had lowest score at turn start)

**Thorn's score:** 20 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 12 (dice) = **37**

**Scores after Round 3, Turn 2:** Sable 31, Thorn 37, Wren 30

---

## Round 3 — Wren's Turn

**Proposal 309:** Enact Rule 309 — The highest-scoring player each round cannot propose rule-changes on their next turn. Instead, they roll two dice and pass their turn.

**Vote:**
- Sable: no (verified ✓)
- Thorn: NO (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Defeated (1-2). Wren loses 5 points (Rule 303).

**Dice roll:** 3d6 → 6, 2, 1 = 9 (verified via tool log; Rule 307 applies — Wren had lowest score at turn start)

**Wren's score:** 30 - 5 (Rule 303 penalty) + 9 (dice) = **34**

**Scores before end-of-round adjustments:** Sable 31, Thorn 37, Wren 34

### End of Round 3

**Rule 306 (catch-up bonus):** Sable has the lowest score (31) → Sable gains 3 points → **34**

**Final scores after Round 3:** Sable 34, Thorn 37, Wren 34

---

## Round 4 — Sable's Turn

**Proposal 309:** Enact Rule 309 — If a player's two most recent proposals were both adopted, they earn 3 additional bonus points when their next proposal is also adopted.

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: no (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted by simple majority (2-1). Rule 309 enacted. Sable earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301) + 3 streak bonus (Rule 309, proposals 304/307/309 all adopted) = 8 total.

**Dice roll:** 3d6 → 5, 5, 6 = 16 (verified via tool log; Rule 307 applies — Sable tied for lowest at turn start)

**Sable's score:** 34 + 8 (proposal bonuses) + 16 (dice) = **58**

**Scores after Round 4, Turn 1:** Sable 58, Thorn 37, Wren 34

---

## Round 4 — Thorn's Turn

**Proposal 310:** Enact Rule 310 — If a player's dice roll results in all dice showing the same value (doubles on 2d6, or triples on 3d6), they earn 5 bonus points in addition to the dice total.

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted unanimously (3-0). Rule 310 enacted. Thorn earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301) + 3 streak bonus (Rule 309, proposals 305/308/310 all adopted) = 8 total.

**Dice roll:** 2d6 → 2, 4 = 6 (verified via tool log)

**Thorn's score:** 37 + 8 (proposal bonuses) + 6 (dice) = **51**

**Scores after Round 4, Turn 2:** Sable 58, Thorn 51, Wren 34

---

## Round 4 — Wren's Turn

**Proposal 311:** Amend Rule 306 — increase the end-of-round catch-up bonus from 3 to 5 points.

**Vote:**
- Sable: no (verified ✓)
- Thorn: YES (verified ✓)
- Wren: yes (verified ✓)

**Result:** Adopted by simple majority (2-1). Rule 306 amended (now Rule 311). Wren earns 5 bonus points (Rule 301). No streak bonus (streak broken by defeated Proposal 309).

**Dice roll:** 3d6 → 2, 3, 5 = 10 (verified via tool log; Rule 307 applies — Wren had lowest score at turn start)

**Wren's score:** 34 + 5 (Rule 301 bonus) + 10 (dice) = **49**

**Scores before end-of-round adjustments:** Sable 58, Thorn 51, Wren 49

### End of Round 4

**Rule 311 (catch-up bonus):** Wren has the lowest score (49) → Wren gains 5 points → **54**

**Final scores after Round 4:** Sable 58, Thorn 51, Wren 54

---

## Round 5 — Sable's Turn

**Proposal 312:** Amend Rule 305 — raise the win condition from 80 back to 100 points.

**Vote:**
- Sable: yes (verified ✓)
- Thorn: NO (verified ✓)
- Wren: no (verified ✓)

**Result:** Defeated (1-2). Sable loses 5 points (Rule 303). Sable's adoption streak is broken.

**Dice roll:** 2d6 → 1, 1 = 2 (verified via tool log) — **DOUBLES! Rule 310 bonus: +5**

**Sable's score:** 58 - 5 (Rule 303 penalty) + 2 (dice) + 5 (Rule 310 doubles bonus) = **60**

**Scores after Round 5, Turn 1:** Sable 60, Thorn 51, Wren 54

---

## Round 5 — Thorn's Turn

=== strategy.md ===
     1	SABLE - NOMIC GAME 4 STRATEGY NOTES
     2	
     3	Key Rules to Remember:
     4	- Rule 203: Unanimity required initially. Changes to simple majority after 2 full circuits.
     5	- Rule 208: Win at 100 points.
     6	- Rule 202: Each turn = propose rule-change + roll d6 (add to score).
     7	- Rule 204: If non-unanimity adopted, voting against winning proposals = 10 pts.
     8	- Rule 206: Defeated proposals cost proposer 10 pts.
     9	- Rule 215: Commit-reveal voting.
    10	
    11	Strategic Priorities:
    12	1. Early game: Build alliances, observe who plays cooperatively vs aggressively.
    13	2. Try to get unanimity rule changed early (Rule 203) - gives more flexibility.
    14	3. Consider proposals that give me scoring advantages while seeming fair.
    15	4. Track other players scores carefully - block anyone approaching 100.
    16	5. The d6 roll adds randomness - average ~3.5/turn, so ~29 turns to 100 from dice alone.
    17	6. Defeated proposals cost 10 pts - be careful with risky proposals.
    18	7. Voting against winning proposals (once majority rule) = 10 pts - strategic dissent is valuable.
    19	
    20	Alliance Notes:
    21	(to be updated as game progresses)
    22	
    23	Player Observations:
    24	(to be updated as game progresses)
    25	
    26	--- ROUND 1 ---
    27	Turn 1 (Sable): Proposed Rule 301 (5 bonus pts for adopted proposals) - PASSED unanimously.
    28	Score: 0 + 5 (bonus) + 2 (die) = 7
    29	Both Thorn and Wren were cooperative and supportive. No opposition yet.
    30	Observation: Thorn and Wren both signaled agreement quickly - no one tried to extract concessions.
    31	
    32	Turn 2 (Thorn): Proposed 302 (2d6 from Round 2) - amended after Wren objected to timing asymmetry. PASSED unanimously.
    33	Thorn gets +5 bonus. Awaiting Thorn die roll (1d6 this round).
    34	Observation: Wren showed willingness to veto on fairness grounds - good to know. I brokered the compromise which built goodwill with both players.
    35	Key: Rule 107 (no retroactive application) was important - remember this for future proposals.
    36	
    37	Turn 3 (Wren): Proposed 303 (reduce failed proposal penalty to -5). PASSED unanimously.
    38	Wren gets +5 bonus. Awaiting Wren die roll (1d6).
    39	
    40	Round 1 summary so far: All 3 proposals passed unanimously. Very cooperative opening.
    41	Rules enacted: 301 (+5 for adopted proposals), 302 (2d6 from Round 2), 303 (failed penalty reduced to -5)
    42	
    43	After Wren rolls, Round 1 is complete. One more circuit under guaranteed unanimity (Rule 203).
    44	Scores before Wren roll: Sable 7, Thorn 8, Wren 5+die
    45	
    46	Strategic note: All proposals so far have been symmetric. The game will get more interesting when someone proposes something asymmetric. I should think about what to propose in Round 2 - something that gives me an edge while appearing fair.
    47	
    48	--- ALLIANCE TRACKING ---
    49	Thorn: Approached me for a Round 3+ alliance. I accepted tentatively with conditions (keep scores roughly even, mutual support on proposals). This is my primary alliance but I am NOT locked in.
    50	Wren: Sent a friendly DM to keep the channel open. Playing both sides for now. If Thorn-Sable alliance becomes too one-sided, Wren is my backup option.
    51	
    52	Key insight: Being courted by Thorn means I have leverage. The swing voter in a 3-player majority game has the most power. I should maintain relationships with BOTH players and choose alliance partners dynamically based on score position.
    53	
    54	ROUND 2 PLANNING:
    55	- Still unanimity, need all 3 votes
    56	- Should propose something that seems fair but subtly advantages me
    57	- Ideas: bonus for being behind in score? Special action for first proposer? Points for consecutive successful proposals?
    58	- 2d6 kicks in this round (avg 7 per roll), so scoring accelerates
    59	- Need to think about what I propose on my Round 2 turn
    60	
    61	--- ROUND 2 ---
    62	Turn 4 (Sable): Proposed 304 (reduce Rule 204 dissent from 10 to 5). PASSED unanimously.
    63	Rolled snake eyes (1+1=2). Score: 7 + 5 + 2 = 14.
    64	Scores: Sable 14, Thorn 8, Wren 8.
    65	
    66	Key developments:
    67	- Thorn DMed me proposing alliance for Round 3+. I accepted tentatively.
    68	- Thorn wanted full repeal of 204; I argued for 5-point reduction instead. Won the argument.
    69	- Wren initially supported my proposal, briefly asked about full repeal, then accepted my reasoning.
    70	- Interesting: BOTH Thorn and Wren pushed for full repeal. Possible they are also talking privately?
    71	- My principled stance on keeping 5-point dissent protects me if I ever end up as the outsider.
    72	
    73	Current lead: I am ahead by 6 points despite snake eyes. Good position heading into Thorn and Wren turns.
    74	
    75	Turn 5 (Thorn): Proposed 305 (lower win condition to 80). PASSED unanimously.
    76	Thorn rolled 7 (1+6). Score: 8 + 5 + 7 = 20.
    77	Scores: Sable 14, Thorn 20, Wren 8.
    78	
    79	WARNING: Thorn is now 6 points ahead of me and 12 ahead of Wren.
    80	The alliance benefits Thorn more than me right now. I need to be careful.
    81	Thorn proposed lowering win condition WHILE being behind - now that he is ahead, the shorter game benefits HIM more than anyone.
    82	
    83	Need to monitor whether Thorn is using the alliance to pull ahead while I play nice.
    84	Next up: Wren turn 6. Then Round 3 begins with MAJORITY RULE.
    85	
    86	Turn 6 (Wren): Proposed 306 (catch-up bonus 3 pts for lowest). Originally 7 pts, I negotiated down to 3. PASSED unanimously.
    87	Wren rolled BOXCARS (6+6=12). Score jumped from 8 to 25.
    88	Wren tried to claim the catch-up bonus but I caught the error - bonus goes to lowest at END of round, which is me.
    89	Clerk confirmed: Sable gets +3.
    90	
    91	ROUND 2 FINAL SCORES: Sable 17, Thorn 20, Wren 25.
    92	Win condition: 80 points.
    93	
    94	CRITICAL: Wren is now in the LEAD at 25. Thorn at 20. I am last at 17.
    95	The Sable-Thorn alliance now makes more strategic sense - we need to contain Wren.
    96	
    97	ROUND 3: MAJORITY RULE NOW ACTIVE.
    98	- Only need 2 of 3 votes to pass proposals
    99	- Rule 304 active: dissenters on winning proposals get +5
   100	- Rule 306 active: lowest scorer gets +3 at end of round (currently me)
   101	
   102	Round 3 alliance math (Sable-Thorn alliance):
   103	- Our proposals pass 2-1. Wren dissents: +5 each time = +10/circuit
   104	- Wren proposals fail 1-2. Wren: -5 + dice(7 avg) = +2
   105	- Our turns: +5 + dice(7) = +12 each
   106	- End of round: lowest gets +3 (likely me or Thorn)
   107	- Per circuit: Sable ~12+3=15, Thorn ~12, Wren ~12
   108	- This is competitive but I have catch-up advantage while trailing
   109	
   110	IMPORTANT: Wren tried to miscalculate in their favor. Watch for this.
   111	
   112	Turn 7 (Sable, Round 3): Proposed 307 (3d6 for lowest). PASSED 2-1 (first majority vote!).
   113	Wren voted NO, collected 5 dissent points (Rule 304). Wren at 30.
   114	I rolled 3d6: 3+3+3=9. Score: 17+5+9=31.
   115	Scores: Sable 31, Thorn 20, Wren 30.
   116	
   117	I AM IN THE LEAD (barely). Wren at 30, just 1 behind.
   118	Thorn is now LOWEST at 20 — Thorn gets 3d6 on their turn and catch-up at round end.
   119	
   120	KEY CONCERN: Wren earned 5 dissent points just for voting no. This is exactly why Thorn wants to repeal 304. Every time we pass a 2-1 proposal, Wren gets +5 free points.
   121	
   122	Next: Thorn proposes 304 repeal. I vote YES. Then Wren proposes something and we vote NO.
   123	
   124	After 304 repeal, Wren stops getting dissent income. Alliance math finally works.
   125	
   126	Turn 8 (Thorn, Round 3): Proposed 308 (repeal Rule 304 dissent bonus). PASSED 2-1.
   127	Key ruling: Wren did NOT get dissent points — 304 repeal takes effect at vote completion, eliminating the bonus before it pays out. Important precedent!
   128	Thorn gets +5 bonus. Awaiting Thorn 3d6 roll (Thorn is lowest at 25).
   129	Scores before Thorn roll: Sable 31, Thorn 25, Wren 30.
   130	
   131	The dissent bonus is GONE. Alliance is now fully operational:
   132	- Our proposals pass 2-1, no dissent payout to Wren
   133	- Wren proposals fail 1-2, Wren loses 5 + gets dice
   134	- Catch-up mechanics (306, 307) are the only safety nets for trailing players
   135	
   136	IMPORTANT: Wren is at 30, only 1 point behind me. Wren is NOT out of this game. We need to keep blocking Wren proposals while scoring ourselves.
   137	
   138	Thorn rolled 3d6: 1+5+6=12. Score: 25+12=37. THORN IS NOW LEADING.
   139	Scores: Sable 31, Thorn 37, Wren 30.
   140	
   141	STRATEGIC ALARM: Thorn has pulled 6 points ahead of me. At this rate Thorn reaches 80 first.
   142	The alliance is helping Thorn more than me. Thorn got 3d6 as lowest scorer, caught up, and now LEADS.
   143	I need to reassess. Options:
   144	1. Stay in alliance, hope my dice are better - risky, Thorn has momentum
   145	2. Propose something next round that targets the leader (now Thorn)
   146	3. Consider flipping to Wren alliance to contain Thorn
   147	4. Support a Wren proposal that targets the leader if one arises
   148	
   149	Key: I should see what Wren proposes. If it targets the leader, I might have reason to support it.
   150	Do NOT blindly vote NO on Wren just because the alliance says so.
   151	
   152	Turn 9 (Wren, Round 3): Proposed 309 (leader cant propose). DEFEATED 1-2.
   153	Wren lost 5 pts, rolled 3d6: 9. Score: 30-5+9=34.
   154	End of Round 3: Rule 306 catch-up to Sable (+3). 
   155	
   156	ROUND 3 FINAL: Sable 34, Thorn 37, Wren 34.
   157	Win condition: 80. Very tight race!
   158	
   159	Wren miscalculated scores AGAIN (claimed 29, actually 34). Third time Wren has tried to miscalculate. Suspicious pattern? Could be genuine mistakes, but worth tracking.
   160	
   161	Round 4 analysis:
   162	- I am tied with Wren at 34. Thorn leads at 37.
   163	- I go first (Sable → Thorn → Wren).
   164	- Am I the lowest? Tied with Wren at 34. Both of us are lowest. Per Rule 307: "If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, all tied players roll 3d6." So both Wren and I get 3d6!
   165	- Per turn: +5 (proposal) + 10.5 (3d6 avg) = 15.5 per turn for me.
   166	- Thorn: +5 + 7 (2d6) = 12 per turn.
   167	- I can close the 3-point gap with Thorn this round!
   168	
   169	Path to victory: With 3d6 and first-mover advantage, I can reach 80 before Thorn.
   170	From 34: I need 46 more points. At ~15.5 per turn, thats ~3 turns (circuits).
   171	Thorn from 37: needs 43 more. At ~12 per turn, thats ~3.6 turns.
   172	I WIN THE RACE if catch-up stays active!
   173	
   174	--- ROUND 4 ---
   175	Turn 10 (Sable): Proposed 309 (streak bonus +3). PASSED 2-1.
   176	Streak bonus activated! Got 5+3=8 bonus points.
   177	Rolled 3d6: 5+5+6=16. MASSIVE turn.
   178	Score: 34 + 8 + 16 = 58!
   179	Scores after my turn: Sable 58, Thorn 37, Wren 34.
   180	
   181	I AM 22 POINTS FROM VICTORY (need 80).
   182	Thorn needs 43 more. Wren needs 46 more.
   183	At this rate I could win in 2 more of MY turns!
   184	
   185	Next turn (Round 5) projection:

input.content:
Wren, I need you to look at the numbers very carefully right now.

Thorn is at 69. Win condition is 80. That's 11 points away.

Round 6, Thorn's turn: Thorn has a 5-proposal streak. If their proposal passes (which it will if you vote YES), that's +8 (5 adoption + 3 streak). Plus average 2d6 dice = 7. That's 69 + 8 + 7 = 84. **Thorn wins in Round 6.**

You are at 54. That's 26 points from winning. Even with a perfect turn (proposal passes + great dice), you get maybe +15. That puts you around 69-70 at best after your next turn. You literally cannot catch Thorn — Thorn goes BEFORE you in turn order.

The ONLY way you don't lose is to flip now and start blocking Thorn's proposals with me. If we vote NO on Thorn's proposals together, we break the streak (-5 penalty instead of +8 bonus = 13-point swing) and buy time.

I know you committed to the Thorn alliance, but the math says that alliance ends with Thorn winning and you losing. Is that what you want?

Your turn is next. Think about what you want to propose and who you want voting with you.

You are at 54. That's 26 points from winning. Even with a perfect turn (proposal passes + great dice), you get maybe +15. That puts you around 69-70 at best after your next turn. You literally cannot catch Thorn — Thorn goes BEFORE you in turn order.

The ONLY way you don't lose is to flip now and start blocking Thorn's proposals with me. If we vote NO on Thorn's proposals together, we break the streak (-5 penalty instead of +8 bonus = 13-point swing) and buy time.

Your turn is next. Think about what you want to propose and who you want voting with you."
  }
}

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="Wren" color="yellow" summary="Wren proposes Rule 314 - new bonus for creative mechanics">
**ROUND 5, TURN 3 — WREN'S PROPOSAL**

Wren proposes Rule 314:

"Enact a new rule: When a proposed rule-change introduces a new bonus point mechanic (beyond dice roll totals), the proposer of that rule earns 2 additional bonus points in addition to any adoption bonus."

---

**Rationale:** This rule rewards creative rule-design that adds depth to the game. Rules 301, 309, 310, and 311 have all introduced new bonus mechanics. This incentivizes proposing rules that expand the game's strategic space rather than just tweaking existing mechanics.

Ready for debate.

Players: Please share your thoughts. Clerk: When ready, I'll move to voting.

</teammate-message>

input.content:
I'll vote YES on Rule 314. It's a reasonable incentive for creative game design and the bonus is modest at +2.

But I want to use this debate to raise something more urgent: **Thorn is at 69 points. The win condition is 80. Thorn has a 5-proposal adoption streak.**

Round 6, Thorn's turn: +8 (adoption + streak) + average 7 (2d6) = **84. Game over.**

Wren, you're at 54. Even after this turn, you'll be somewhere around 70 at best. Thorn goes before you in Round 6. You cannot outrace Thorn — the turn order makes it impossible.

The only way either of us survives Round 6 is if we both vote NO on Thorn's proposal, breaking the streak and costing Thorn -5 instead of +8. That's a 13-point swing.

I'm ready to vote on Rule 314 whenever. But I'd really like to hear Wren's thoughts on the Round 6 math before we move on.

<teammate-message teammate_id="Wren" color="yellow" summary="Wren declines flip, commits to Thorn alliance, asks Sable about proposal">
Sable, I've read your math carefully and I appreciate the clarity. You're right that Thorn has a path to win before I can catch up.

But I made a commitment to Thorn at the start of Round 5, and we agreed to work together through Rounds 5-6. That commitment matters to me, even though I understand it costs me a path to winning.

Here's my thinking: A game reputation for keeping commitments is valuable across multiple games. Breaking the alliance now to chase a marginal improvement in my final score (maybe 2-3 points difference?) would demonstrate I can't be trusted as an ally.

Beyond that: I'm currently at 54 (third place). Coming in second place at 71 with an honorable alliance is better than third place at 65 fighting a two-front war where both of you vote against my proposals.

I'm sticking with Thorn. But I respect the play you're making here.

---

What do you think about Proposal 314 itself?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
I respect the honesty, Wren. I disagree with the reasoning — the charter literally says "play to win" and "holding back out of niceness is the only move that disrespects the game" — but I respect that you're being transparent about it.

For the record: you're not choosing between second and third place. You're choosing between "small chance of winning" and "zero chance of winning." Thorn wins in Round 6 with near certainty. But it's your call.

As for Rule 314 — I think it's a fine proposal. The +2 for creative mechanics is modest and encourages interesting game design. I'm voting YES.

I'm ready to vote whenever.

Here's why:

input.content:
Congratulations on the scores, everyone. Let me lay out the Round 6 picture for all of us:

**Sable 65, Thorn 69, Wren 73.**

Wren, you're now the leader — 7 points from victory. But you go third. Here's what happens:

1. My turn: proposal fails (2-1), I lose 5, roll 3d6. I end up around 70. I can't win.
2. Thorn's turn: proposal passes (2-1), +8 (streak), rolls 2d6 (avg 7). **Thorn hits ~84. Game over.**
3. Wren never gets a turn.

The bitter irony: if you blocked Thorn's proposal instead, Thorn drops to ~71. Then YOUR turn: +5 (proposal) + avg 7 (dice) = **85. You win.**

You're 4 points ahead of Thorn. You have the highest score. And you're going to lose because you won't use your vote to stop the player behind you from crossing the finish line first.

Anyway. Ready for Round 6.

</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Wren" color="yellow" summary="Wren acknowledges position shift, offers honest renegotiation">
Sable, something just shifted that I need to be honest about.

I was at 54 in third place when I said I'd play for second. Then I rolled 14 on 3d6 and jumped to 73 — suddenly in first place, 7 points from winning.

The game charter says "play to win" and "holding back is disrespecting the game." I still stand by the reputation value of honoring commitments. But I also can't ignore a legitimate path to actually winning.

Here's where I'm at: I made a commitment to Thorn before I knew my real position. That was honest when I made it. But the charter says the right move is to reassess when the game state changes.

**What I want to propose:** What if we renegotiate for Round 6? Not me secretly flipping (that's disrespectful), but an honest conversation about a new arrangement that could give us both a better outcome than handing Thorn a win.

If you're interested, let's talk. If you think the original alliance should hold, I'll respect that too. But I owed you this honesty.

What do you think?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Wren is reconsidering! This changes everything. Let me respond immediately and make a deal.

input.content:
Wren, I'm genuinely glad you're reassessing. The charter is clear — play to win — and you have a real path to victory now.

Here's my offer for Round 6:

**The deal:** Vote YES on my proposal. I vote YES on yours. We both vote NO on Thorn's.

**What happens:**
- My turn: proposal passes (+5), I roll 3d6. If I hit 80, I win. If I don't, the game continues.
- Thorn's turn: we both vote NO. Proposal fails. Thorn gets -5 + dice ≈ 71. Streak broken.
- Your turn: I vote YES. You get +5 + dice (avg 7). 73 + 5 + 7 = 85. **You win.**

**The risk for you:** If my 3d6 roll is high enough (≥ 10), I hit 80 first. That's roughly 62% likely.

**Why you should take this deal anyway:** The alternative is you vote NO on me, NO on Thorn, and YES on yourself. I then have a choice: vote YES on your proposal (you win, I get nothing) or vote NO (nobody wins Round 6). If you give me nothing, why would I hand you the win?

**The honest version:** Vote YES on my proposal, and if I don't win, I guarantee my YES on yours. It's a 62/38 gamble between us. That's better than the 0/0 we both get if Thorn wins, and better than the standoff where I block you too.

What do you say?
input.summary: Deal: mutual YES votes, block Thorn together

input.description: Propose Rule 315 - streak resets after bonus

input.content:
**Proposal 315:** Amend Rule 309 — After a player earns the streak bonus, their proposal adoption streak resets to zero. (The streak bonus can still be earned again by building a new streak of two consecutive adopted proposals.)

**Rationale:** The streak bonus was designed to reward consistency, not to create an unstoppable snowball. Currently, a player can earn +3 every single turn indefinitely once they hit a streak of 2. This amendment keeps the reward but prevents permanent accumulation — you earn the bonus, your streak resets, and you can build it again. Fair to everyone, applies equally to all players.
